ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid food product class; no specific garlic butter preparation exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers squid and cuttlefish as a category; no garlic butter preparation sub-code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for garlic butter whole squid as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of garlic butter whole squid is approximately 300 g, representing one whole medium-to-large squid dressed with garlic butter sauce, as served in a Zi Char or Malaysian seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Squid, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15150); butter and garlic contribution estimated from USDA Foundation Foods entries for unsalted butter (NDB 01001) and raw garlic (NDB 11215), proportioned at approximately 85% squid, 10% butter, 5% garlic by weight
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for garlic butter whole squid exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy cooked squid (NDB 15150) as the base (85% by weight) with USDA Foundation Foods unsalted butter (NDB 01001) at 10% and raw garlic (NDB 11215) at 5%. Energy and macros are weighted composites. This is a medium-confidence estimate. IMR Malaysia MyFCD and HPB Foodo were checked and do not contain a matching garlic butter squid entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; squid / seafood analogous estimate; protein-dominant seafood foods typically GI 30–50
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for garlic butter whole squid exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or published literature. GI is estimated at 40 based on the analogous category of cooked seafood / squid, which is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food. Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN) international GI tables confirm that protein-dominant seafood dishes have GI in the range 30–50. Garlic and butter contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 8.4) / 100 = 3.4, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 2.8 × 3 = 8.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from butter and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of garlic butter whole squid provides approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 36–42% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Garlic butter whole squid has a low estimated GI of 40 and provides only approximately 8.4 g of carbohydrate per 300 g serving, resulting in a glycemic load of 3.4, making it a suitable protein-rich choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol and saturated fat from butter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 300 g serving contributes approximately 585 mg dietary cholesterol and 13.5 g saturated fat primarily from butter; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and consider requesting the dish prepared with reduced butter or olive oil substitution.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Garlic Butter Whole Squid is a popular Zi Char and Malaysian seafood restaurant dish in which a whole fresh squid is cleaned, scored or left intact, and cooked — typically by grilling, wok-frying, or pan-searing — then generously basted or tossed in a sauce of melted butter, minced garlic, salt, pepper, and sometimes spring onion or chilli. The result is a dish with a rich, savoury, and aromatic flavour profile: the squid flesh is tender and slightly chewy with a mild oceanic sweetness, while the garlic butter sauce provides a deeply fragrant, umami-laden coating. In Singapore and Malaysia, this dish is a staple of Zi Char (煮炒) menus — informal Chinese-style cook-to-order seafood stalls — and is commonly ordered as a shared dish alongside rice. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of lean protein from the squid (approximately 13 g per 100 g), moderate in fat due to the butter component (approximately 7.8 g per 100 g, of which 4.5 g is saturated), and low in carbohydrates (approximately 2.8 g per 100 g). It provides meaningful amounts of vitamin B12, selenium, phosphorus, and cholesterol. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on preparation, and individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should exercise portion awareness. The dish is not suitable for vegans or those with dairy or mollusc allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in garlic butter whole squid?
Is garlic butter squid suitable for diabetics?
What is Zi Char garlic squid?
Can I eat garlic butter squid if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for garlic butter whole squid exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composition of USDA SR Legacy cooked squid (NDB 15150), USDA Foundation Foods unsalted butter (NDB 01001), and raw garlic (NDB 11215) at approximate proportions of 85:10:5 by weight. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed international GI tables. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy squid entry (NDB 15150) and is not reliably quantified in the composite estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are estimated from the weighted USDA component sources and should be treated as approximate. The halal status is marked false because butter from non-certified sources may not be halal-certified; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. Garlic butter whole squid is a Zi Char dish common in Singapore and Malaysia; the cuisine tag 'Crab' in the input source data appears to be a platform categorisation artefact and does not reflect the dish content. GI set to 40 as an analogous estimate for a high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked seafood dish per Atkinson et al. 2021 (AJCN) international GI tables; gi_category is low (GI ≤ 55). GL per serving = (40 × 8.4) / 100 = 3.4 for a 300 g serving.
